Great work! You understand this well.
Amazing! You are a chemistry star!
Nice! That bond works.
That bond does not work here. Think about how many electrons each atom needs.
Mission complete! You did it!
Hydrogen atoms share one pair of electrons to make a single bond.
Hydrogen has 1 electron to share. Oxygen needs 2 more to be happy.
Carbon can make 4 bonds. Hydrogen makes 1.
Nitrogen has 5 valence electrons and usually makes 3 bonds.
Chlorine has 7 valence electrons and needs 1 more to fill its outer shell.
Carbon and oxygen can share one pair of electrons.
Carbon and oxygen can share two pairs of electrons for a double bond.
Carbon atoms can bond to each other, making chains and rings.
Nitrogen gas has a triple bond—three pairs of shared electrons. That is why it is so strong!
Sodium gives its electron to chlorine. Now Na is positive and Cl is negative. Opposites attract!
Magnesium gives 2 electrons to oxygen. Both become charged ions.
Water forms when oxygen shares electrons with two hydrogen atoms. Oxygen gets the electrons it needs, and each hydrogen gets one too.
Methane has one carbon in the center bonded to four hydrogens. Carbon makes 4 bonds, and each hydrogen makes 1.
Carbon dioxide has double bonds between carbon and each oxygen. Double bonds mean two pairs of electrons are shared.
Sodium chloride is an ionic compound. Sodium loses an electron to become Na+, and chlorine gains it to become Cl-. The opposite charges attract.
In every chemical reaction, the same number of each type of atom exists before and after. Atoms are just rearranged!
Ammonia has one nitrogen bonded to three hydrogens. Nitrogen has 5 valence electrons and shares 3 of them.
Nitrogen gas has a triple bond. Each nitrogen shares 3 electrons, giving both a full outer shell of 8.

This atom cannot make any more bonds right now.
These atoms do not form this type of bond.
The number of atoms is not the same on both sides. Count again!
Start with one H2 molecule and one Cl2 molecule on the left. On the right, make two HCl molecules.
You need 1 CH4 and 2 O2 on the left. On the right, make 1 CO2 and 2 H2O. Count every atom carefully!
Place 1 N2 and 3 H2 on the left. On the right, build 2 NH3 molecules. Check that nitrogen and hydrogen atoms balance.
Hydrogen chloride forms when hydrogen and chlorine gases combine. Each H2 and Cl2 molecule breaks apart, and the atoms rearrange into HCl molecules. The total number of H and Cl atoms stays the same!
When methane burns, the carbon joins with oxygen to make CO2, and the hydrogens join with oxygen to make water. This is why burning natural gas produces both carbon dioxide and water vapor.
The Haber-Bosch process combines nitrogen and hydrogen to make ammonia, which is used to make fertilizers. It takes 1 N2 and 3 H2 to make 2 NH3, and every atom is accounted for!
Hydrogen gas is two hydrogen atoms sharing one pair of electrons. Each H starts with just 1 electron and gets a full pair by sharing. It is the simplest molecule there is.
Chlorine gas is two chlorine atoms sharing one pair of electrons. Each Cl needs one more electron to fill its outer shell, and sharing gets both atoms there.
Oxygen gas is two oxygen atoms sharing two pairs of electrons—a double bond. That gives each oxygen a full outer shell of 8 electrons.
Hydrogen chloride is one hydrogen sharing electrons with one chlorine. H needs 1 more electron and Cl needs 1 more, so a single bond gives both what they need.
Carbon monoxide has carbon and oxygen sharing three pairs of electrons—a triple bond. That is one of the strongest bonds in chemistry.
Hydrogen peroxide is H–O–O–H. The two oxygens share a single bond with each other, and each oxygen is also bonded to a hydrogen. It is like water with an extra oxygen.
Two hydrogen molecules and one oxygen molecule rearrange into two water molecules. The 4 H and 2 O atoms are the same on both sides—just regrouped.
Carbon monoxide and oxygen rearrange into carbon dioxide. Two CO and one O2 turn into 2 CO2. Every C and every O is still there, just put together differently.
Water can be split apart with energy. Two water molecules become two hydrogen molecules and one oxygen molecule. Same atoms, different arrangement.
Hydrogen peroxide breaks down into water and oxygen. Two H2O2 molecules become two H2O and one O2. The atoms balance on both sides.

Sodium hydroxide is a strong base. Sodium gives its electron to oxygen (an ionic bond), and the oxygen still holds onto a hydrogen (a covalent bond). The whole molecule is one ionic + one covalent bond working together.
Ethane is two carbons sharing a bond, with three hydrogens on each side. C-C bonds are how carbon builds chains and rings — the foundation of every organic molecule, from sugars to plastics.

An acid (HCl) and a base (NaOH) react to make a salt (NaCl) and water. The H+ from the acid joins the OH- from the base — that's why neutralization always produces water. Every atom is accounted for on both sides.
Sodium gives its electron to oxygen — an ionic bond. The oxygen is part of an OH group with the hydrogen.
